← Back to Deep SummariesFull text ↗

HEAX: An Architecture for Computing on Encrypted Data

ASPLOS 2020 (ACM) · September 2019 · full view ↗

Motivation and Contributions

Cloud computing introduces serious data-privacy and confidentiality risks, and regulations such as GDPR, HIPAA, and CCPA make it impossible for some data owners to outsource computation in the clear. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE), introduced by Gentry in 2009, lets a cloud server evaluate arbitrary functions directly on ciphertexts without ever decrypting them, giving end-to-end encryption where only the client (holding the secret key) can recover results. The central obstacle to deploying FHE at scale is its enormous computation overhead compared to plaintext computation.

HEAX (Homomorphic Encryption Acceleration) is a hardware architecture targeting this overhead. The paper focuses on the CKKS scheme, which performs approximate computation on real / fixed-point numbers and supports efficient truncation, making it the prime candidate for machine learning on encrypted data (for example oblivious neural-network inference in MLaaS). The authors are M. Sadegh Riazi (UC San Diego), Kim Laine, Blake Pelton, and Wei Dai (Microsoft / Microsoft Research); the work appeared at ASPLOS 2020 in Lausanne, Switzerland.

Figure 1 shows the end-to-end usage model: the client encodes and encrypts private data and ships ciphertexts and evaluation keys to the server, which performs Addition, Multiplication, KeySwitching, Relinearization, and Rotation on encrypted data; the client later decrypts and decodes the result. Encoding / decoding and encryption / decryption are cheap and stay on the client side, so HEAX accelerates only the computationally intensive server-side operations.

Three main contributions:

  • A novel, highly parallelizable architecture for the Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT) that processes arbitrary-sized polynomials with adjustable throughput. NTT is the usual bottleneck across lattice-based cryptosystems, so the design is of independent interest.
  • The first hardware architecture for the CKKS homomorphic encryption scheme, leveraging multi-layer parallelism from the ciphertext level down to fine-grained modular-arithmetic engines, and scalable across different FPGA chips because it is not custom-built for one fixed parameter set.
  • A proof-of-concept on two Intel FPGAs (Arria 10 and Stratix 10), implementing all high-level CKKS operations and evaluated across three FHE parameter sets, demonstrating more than two orders of magnitude speedup over the heavily optimized Microsoft SEAL library on CPU.

Design Challenges and Prior Art

A ciphertext in FHE is a set (usually a pair) of polynomials of degree $n-1$ (vectors of $n$ integers) modulo a large integer. Homomorphic operations involve computationally intensive modular arithmetic on big integers (hundreds of bits) with convoluted data dependencies, and the polynomial degree $n$ is large (thousands), so storing entire intermediate results on the FPGA chip is prohibitive.

Prior FPGA work for non-CKKS schemes took one of three approaches, each with drawbacks: (i) co-processors that accelerate only low-level ring operations while high-level operations stay on the CPU, limiting practical use; (ii) storing intermediate results in off-chip memory, which degrades performance so severely it can be worse than naive software execution; or (iii) hardware fixed to a single modest parameter (for example $n = 2^{12}$), even though encryption parameters determine both security level and multiplication depth and are application dependent. A primary HEAX design goal is therefore an architecture usable across a wide range of parameters, together with techniques to efficiently store / access data on-chip and minimize (or for some parameter sets eliminate) off-chip memory accesses.

CKKS Preliminaries: Notation, RNS, Gadget Decomposition

CKKS supports homomorphic addition (cheap: add corresponding polynomials) and multiplication (expensive: it increases the number of polynomial components, requiring relinearization to bring the ciphertext back to a pair). To keep underlying plaintext magnitudes from blowing up, a rescaling operation divides the plaintext value by a constant. SIMD-style packing embeds many numbers in one ciphertext, and rotation permutes those numbers. Relinearization, rescaling, and rotation can all be expressed as a unified operation called Key Switching plus pre / post-processing.

Notation. Integers and reals are in normal case; polynomials and vectors are in bold ($\mathbf{a}$); vectors of polynomials and matrices are in upper-case bold ($\mathbf{A}$), with $\mathbf{a}_i$ the $i$-th polynomial or row. $n$ is a power of two, and the polynomial ring is $R = \mathbb{Z}[X]/(X^n + 1)$, whose elements have degree at most $n-1$ since $X^n = -1 \in R$. Write $R_q = R/qR$ for the residue ring modulo integer $q$; coefficients live in $[-\lfloor (q-1)/2 \rfloor, \lfloor q/2 \rfloor] \cap \mathbb{Z}$ but are stored in $[0, q-1] \cap \mathbb{Z}$. The product $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ is reduced modulo $X^n+1$ in $R$ and further modulo $q$ in $R_q$; $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \sum_i \mathbf{u}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_i$ is the dot product, and $\mathbf{u} \odot \mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{u}_0 \cdot \mathbf{v}_0,\, \mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1,\, \dots)$ is the coefficient-wise (dyadic) product. For a real $r$, $\lfloor r \rceil$ is the nearest integer and $\lfloor r \rfloor$ the floor; for an integer $a$, $[a]_p$ denotes reduction modulo $p$ into $[0, p-1] \cap \mathbb{Z}$. Sampling $a$ from distribution $\chi$ is written $a \leftarrow \chi$, and $U(S)$ is the uniform distribution on a finite set $S$.

Residue Number System (RNS). Choosing $q = \prod_{i=0}^{L} p_i$ with the $p_i$ pairwise coprime lets arithmetic over $R_q$ be carried out independently and in parallel on each RNS basis $R_{p_i}$ via the ring isomorphism in Equation 1:

$$ R_q \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; \prod_{i=0}^{L} R_{p_i}, \qquad a \;\longmapsto\; \big( a_i = [a]_{p_i} \big)_{0 \le i \le L} $$

The inverse mapping is $a = \sum_{i=0}^{L} a_i\, \pi_i \, \big[\pi_i^{-1}\big]_{p_i} \pmod{q}$ with $\pi_i = q/p_i$. Additions and multiplications in $R_q$, written $c = \mathrm{Func}(a,b)$, then become $c_i = \mathrm{Func}(a_i, b_i)$ in each $R_{p_i}$ in parallel for $i = 0, 1, \dots, L$. HEAX exploits this RNS-level parallelism extensively; the full-RNS variant of CKKS underlies the design.

Gadget decomposition. For a gadget vector $\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{Z}^d$ and integer $q$, the RNS-friendly gadget decomposition $g^{-1}$ maps an element $a \in R_q$ into a vector $\mathbf{A} \in R^d$ of small polynomials such that $a = \langle \mathbf{g}, \mathbf{A} \rangle \pmod{q}$.

CKKS subroutines. $\mathrm{Setup}(\lambda)$ fixes ring size $n$, ciphertext modulus $q$, a special modulus $p$ coprime to $q$, a key distribution $\chi$, and an error distribution $\Omega$. $\mathrm{SymEnc}(\mathbf{m}, \mathbf{s})$ samples $\mathbf{a} \leftarrow U(R_{qp})$ and $\mathbf{e} \leftarrow \Omega$, sets $\mathbf{b} = -\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{s} + \mathbf{e}$, and returns $\mathrm{ct} = (\mathbf{c}_0, \mathbf{c}_1) = (\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{a})$. $\mathrm{KeyGen}$ samples $\mathbf{s} \leftarrow \chi$ and returns secret key $\mathbf{s}$ and public key $\mathrm{SymEnc}(0, \mathbf{s})$. $\mathrm{KskGen}(\mathbf{s}', \mathbf{s})$ returns a key-switching key $\mathrm{ksk} = (\mathbf{D}_0 \mid \mathbf{D}_1) \in R_{q_\ell p}^{(L+2)\times 2}$ with $(\mathbf{d}_{0,i}, \mathbf{d}_{1,i}) \leftarrow \mathrm{SymEnc}(g_i \cdot \mathbf{s}', \mathbf{s})$. $\mathrm{Add}(\mathrm{ct}_0, \mathrm{ct}_1)$ adds the polynomial pairs. Two frequently used low-level primitives are modular reduction $\mathrm{Mod}(x, p)$ (Barrett-style, with precomputed $u = \lfloor 2^{2w}/p \rfloor$, valid for $x \in [0, (p-1)^2]$) and the optimized modular multiplication $\mathrm{MulRed}$ given in the next section.

Optimized Modular Multiplication (Algorithm 1)

The core fine-grained arithmetic primitive. For $w$-bit words and a modulus $p < 2^{w-2}$, given $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ and a precomputed $y' = \lfloor y \cdot 2^w / p \rfloor$, it computes $x \cdot y \bmod p$ using only single-word operations and at most one conditional subtraction:

Algorithm 1  Optimized Modular Mult.  |  MulRed(x, y, y', p)
Input:  x, y in Z_p ;  p < 2^(w-2) ;  y' = floor( y . 2^w / p )
Output: z = x . y  (mod p)
  1:  z   <-  x . y          (mod 2^w)      // lower word of the product
  2:  t   <-  floor( x . y' / 2^w )         // upper word of the product
  3:  z_e <-  t . p          (mod 2^w)      // lower word of the product
  4:  z   <-  z - z_e                       // single-word subtraction
  5:  if z >= p then
  6:      z <-  z - p
  7:  end if
  8:  return z

The trick is that $t = \lfloor x\,y'/2^w \rfloor$ approximates the quotient $\lfloor x y / p \rfloor$ to within one, so $z - t\,p$ lands in $[0, 2p)$ and a single subtraction corrects it.

MULT Module and Word Size

Homomorphic multiplication is performed in RNS and NTT form. Although ciphertexts can in general have more than two components, in practice they are relinearized and multiplied on two components; the HEAX MULT architecture is nonetheless generic for any number of components. Homomorphic multiplication is given in Algorithm 2: inputs are two ciphertexts $\mathrm{ct}_0 = (\tilde{\mathbf{A}}_0, \tilde{\mathbf{A}}_1)$ and $\mathrm{ct}_1 = (\tilde{\mathbf{B}}_0, \tilde{\mathbf{B}}_1)$ and the output is a three-component ciphertext, computed independently on each RNS basis. Since ciphertexts are kept in NTT form by default, homomorphic multiplication reduces to a series of coefficient-wise (dyadic, $\odot$) products over components followed by modular reduction.

Word size. SEAL uses 64-bit native operations for x86, but on FPGAs the DSP bit-width varies (the two boards here have 27-bit DSPs). A naive 64-bit multiplier needs nine 27-bit DSPs, whereas a 54-bit multiplier needs only four. Reducing RNS-base bit-width may increase the number of bases by roughly $\tfrac{64}{54} \approx 1.2\times$, but in practice small ciphertext moduli are under 54 bits so no extra moduli are needed. (More elaborate Toom-Cook multiplication can implement 64-bit multiply with five 27-bit multipliers plus extra bit-level and add operations.) Switching from 64-bit to 54-bit native operations yields a $1.4\times$ to $2.25\times$ reduction in DSP count depending on HE parameters. To support 54-bit words, all moduli $p_i$ must be (i) less than 52 bits to keep Algorithm 1 correct and (ii) congruent to $1 \bmod 2n$ to support NTT; SEAL was modified and such moduli precomputed for every parameter set.

MULT architecture. The MULT module (Figure 2) handles both ciphertext-ciphertext (C-C) and ciphertext-plaintext (C-P, a special case) multiplication. It contains $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}}$ Dyadic Cores, computing $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}}$ dyadic multiplications per clock cycle. Each Dyadic core takes two coefficients ($\mathrm{Op}_1$, $\mathrm{Op}_2$), two precomputed constants ($R_1$, $R_2$), and the one-word prime $p$. For ciphertexts with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ components, the output has $\alpha + \beta - 1$ components. Storing only one residue of one component would minimize BRAM but blow up data transfer from $(\alpha+\beta)\cdot n$ to $(\alpha\cdot\beta + \min(\alpha,\beta))\cdot n$ words; instead HEAX allocates $\alpha$ memories of size $n$ for $\mathrm{ct}_0$ and $\beta$ for $\mathrm{ct}_1$, achieving $O\big((\alpha+\beta)\cdot n\big)$ data transfer and BRAM consumption. Because each BRAM supports only one read and one write per cycle, each polynomial is stored across $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}}$ parallel memory blocks sharing common read / write address signals; one row across these BRAMs is a memory element (ME). Each cycle one ME is read from $\mathrm{ct}_0$ and $\mathrm{ct}_1$ banks (ME1 / ME2) and the result (ME3) is written to a separate output memory.

NTT and Flooring Algorithms (Algorithms 3 and 4)

NTT and its inverse (INTT) are the most computationally intensive low-level operations. Polynomial multiplication is done via the convolution theorem: $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \in R_p$ corresponds to the negacyclic convolution of the coefficient vectors in $\mathbb{Z}_p^n$, given in Equation 2:

$$ c_j \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{j} a_i\, b_{j-i} \;-\; \sum_{i=j+1}^{n-1} a_i\, b_{j-i+n} \pmod{p}, \qquad j = 0, 1, \dots, n-1 $$

For a ring degree $n$, a prime $p \equiv 1 \bmod 2n$ is chosen so that a $2n$-th primitive root of unity $\psi$ exists ($\psi^n \equiv -1 \bmod p$). The forward transform $\tilde{\mathbf{a}} = \mathrm{NTT}_p(\mathbf{a})$ is defined in Equation 3:

$$ \tilde{a}_j \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} a_i\, \psi^{(2i+1)j} $$

Polynomials are kept in NTT form to reduce the number of conversions. HEAX adapts the fast negacyclic-convolution NTT of Longa and Naehrig. The forward NTT (Algorithm 3) consumes powers of $\psi$ stored in bit-reverse order ($\mathbf{Y}$, with precomputed $\mathbf{Y}' = \lfloor \mathbf{Y}\cdot 2^w / p \rfloor$) and produces a bit-reversed output via in-place butterfly updates that each call $\mathrm{MulRed}$ once (the NTT Core).

A related operation used during key switching and rescaling is flooring (Algorithm 4), which divides by the special prime $p$ in the RNS / NTT domain, producing the RNS / NTT form of $\mathbf{c}' = \lfloor p^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{c} \rfloor \in R_{q_\ell}$ given $\tilde{\mathbf{C}} = (\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_0, \dots, \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{\ell+1})$, the RNS / NTT form of $\mathbf{c} \in R_{q_\ell p}$. Both algorithms are spelled out in full in the Algorithms appendix.

NTT Architecture and Multi-Level Parallelism

HEAX uses NTT to mean both NTT and INTT modules. In KeySwitch (Algorithm 5) NTT is used in several places with differing throughput needs, so HEAX allocates one NTT module per NTT operation and makes each module's throughput adjustable via its number of cores $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$. The module computes an $n$-point NTT in $\log n$ stages, each stage taking $\tfrac{n}{2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}}$ steps; the full NTT takes $\tfrac{n \log n}{2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}}$ cycles. Figure 3 shows the full NTT module with its data memory, twiddle-factor memories, output memory, the multiplexer network, and NTT cores.

NTT Core. Each core accepts two coefficients ($c_{\mathrm{in}}.a$, $c_{\mathrm{in}}.b$), one twiddle factor $w$, one precomputed value $w_p$, and a prime $p$, and outputs two transformed coefficients ($c_{\mathrm{out}}.a$, $c_{\mathrm{out}}.b$). All modular operations inside the core are pipelined. At full utilization, $2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ coefficients are transformed per clock cycle. To read / write that many coefficients per cycle, each polynomial is stored across many parallel BRAMs sharing read / write addresses, exploiting the fact that NTT's number of consecutive accesses is always a power of two. One ME is fetched per cycle into even / odd registers ($\mathrm{ME}_e$, $\mathrm{ME}_o$), and a multiplexer network routes the correct coefficients to each core input.

Access patterns (Type 1 vs Type 2). During the first $(\log n - \log \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}} - 1)$ stages (Type 1), the paired coefficients for each NTT core lie in different MEs, so two MEs must be fetched before computation, naively introducing a 50% pipeline bubble that drops throughput to $(\log n - \log \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}} - 1)/\log n$. In general at stage $i$, coefficient $x[j+m]$ is paired with $x\big[j + m + \tfrac{n}{2^{1+i}}\big]$. Once $\tfrac{n}{2^i}$ reaches $2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ the inter-ME dependency vanishes and pairs come from within one ME (Type 2), but coefficients must be reordered to reach the right core. Figure 4 illustrates the Type 1 and Type 2 access patterns for $n = 16$. Twiddle factors have their own pattern: at stage $i$ only $2^i$ unique values are used; they are stored in batches of size $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ in parallel.

Optimal multiplexers. Naive reordering would use $2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ multiplexers each selecting from $2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ inputs ($\mathrm{MUX}_{2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}}$), which consumes huge register / logic resources and fails place-and-route beyond about 32 cores. Instead HEAX observes that each NTT-core input draws from at most $\log \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ distinct indices, so it uses $(4\cdot\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}})$ multiplexers of size at most $\mathrm{MUX}_{\log 2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}}$. For an 8-core NTT module this reduces register usage from $224{,}000$ to $97{,}000$. Since the register is the most limited resource for the NTT module, this optimization is essential to scale the design.

Full pipeline utilization. To remove the Type-1 bubble, HEAX doubles the ME size (storing $2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}}$ consecutive coefficients per ME) and halves memory depth, so two MEs can be transformed in the next two cycles after the two reads, yielding full NTT-core utilization. All reads / writes across stages are in-place, so no extra BRAM stores intermediates.

Memory utilization and word-packing. M20K units hold 512 words of 40 bits and serve one access per cycle. As long as $\tfrac{n}{\beta} \ge 512$, M20K is fully depth-utilized (only marginal at $n = 2^{12}$, $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}} = 16$, which is not BRAM-constrained). Width-wise, storing one 54-bit coefficient per BRAM only reaches $\tfrac{54}{2\cdot 40} = 68\%$ utilization; packing $\beta$ coefficients reaches $\beta\cdot 54 / (\lceil \beta\cdot 54/40 \rceil \cdot 40)$, exceeding $98\%$ for $\beta = 8$, which matters because at $n = 2^{14}$ the design becomes BRAM-constrained and must spill to DRAM. The INTT module is identical to NTT except the INTT core replaces the NTT core, the control unit runs stages in reverse, and twiddle factors are the inverse ones.

Key Switching Algorithm (Algorithm 5)

Key switching makes a ciphertext decryptable under a different secret key, homomorphically. It is the most computationally intensive high-level CKKS operation and underlies relinearization and rotation. HEAX uses a gadget decomposition (with the special modulus $p$ and a rescaling at the end) that yields fast, almost noise-free key switching. The procedure (Algorithm 5) takes $\mathrm{ct} = (\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_0, \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_1)$ and the key-switching key $\mathrm{ksk} = (\mathbf{D}_0 \mid \mathbf{D}_1)$ and proceeds in four phases marked in the listing: an INTT to leave NTT form, NTTs into every other prime, dyadic products against the relinearization / Galois keys with accumulation, and a final flooring (modulus switch) plus a $\mathrm{CKKS.Add}$ with the original ciphertext. Here $k = L+1$ is the number of RNS components of the ciphertext modulus; because each INTT must be re-NTT'd into the other $k$ primes plus the special modulus, the NTT phase carries roughly $k$ times the throughput of the leading INTT. The full numbered listing appears in the Algorithms appendix.

KeySwitch Architecture and Throughput Balancing

KeySwitch is designed as a fully end-to-end pipelined module that processes many key-switching operations simultaneously without excessive FIFOs (Figure 5). To reduce on-chip memory, the design takes one polynomial (one RNS component) at a time and outputs two polynomials. Since all polynomials are in NTT form by default, the input is first converted back via the first INTT module ($\mathrm{INTT}_0$), then transformed to NTT form for all other primes including the special modulus. Because each INTT requires $k$ NTTs ($k = L+1$), the NTT throughput must be $k$ times that of $\mathrm{INTT}_0$. This is realized either by one NTT module with $k$ times more cores, or by multiple NTT modules with fewer cores each (this NTT block is denoted $\mathrm{NTT}_0$). After NTT, the DyadMult module multiplies by the relinearization / Galois keys; a separate Dyadic module also handles the dyadic product on the original input polynomial.

The flow repeats $k$ times (one per RNS component), accumulating results in two sets of BRAM banks (one per output polynomial). Then Modulus Switching (executing Floor) transforms the special-modulus polynomial back to the time domain ($\mathrm{INTT}_1$) and forward under every other prime ($\mathrm{NTT}_1$), multiplies by the inverse prime, and subtracts; the MS module embeds the multiply and subtract. Outputs are two sets of $k$ polynomials (Output Poly 0 / 1).

Throughput balancing. With $m_0$ instances of $\mathrm{NTT}_0$, $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_0} = k \cdot \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_0} / m_0$. An NTT takes $\tfrac{n \log n}{2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_0}}$ cycles while a dyadic multiplication on two key sets takes $\tfrac{2n}{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}}}$ cycles. Requiring the Dyadic throughput to meet or exceed NTT's gives Equation 4:

$$ \frac{2n}{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}}} \;\le\; \frac{n \log n}{2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_0}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{DYD}} \;=\; \frac{4\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_0}}{\log n} $$

Similarly $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_1} = \lceil \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_0} / k \rceil$, $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_1} = \mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_0}$, and $\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{MS}} = \lceil 2\,\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_1}/\log n \rceil$. Optimal per-board parameters are summarized in the paper's Table 5 (see Implementation).

Pipeline and synchronization. All modules and their internal components are pipelined and throughput-balanced, so multiple key switches occupy different pipeline stages simultaneously (Figure 6). The fifth Dyadic module (on the input polynomial) is synchronized with the others to avoid memory replication of the large key banks, which creates Data Dependency 1: by the time the $k$-th Dyadic module starts, the input polynomial may be overwritten by the next operation, so HEAX allocates $f_1$ polynomial buffers as in Equation 5:

$$ f_1 \;=\; \left\lceil 3 + \frac{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_0}}{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_0}} \right\rceil $$

Data Dependency 2 arises because MS consumes DyadMult outputs, requiring $f_2$ buffers as in Equation 6:

$$ f_2 \;=\; \left\lceil 1 + m_0 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_1}}{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{NTT}_1}} + \frac{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{INTT}_1} \cdot \log n}{\mathrm{nc}_{\mathrm{MS}}} \right\rceil $$

System View, Memory, and Data Flow

The system comprises a host CPU and an FPGA board connected over PCIe; the board holds the FPGA chip and off-chip DRAM over a DDR interface (Figure 7). On the FPGA chip sit the MULT, KeySwitch, NTT, and INTT modules plus a control unit and shell. The host runs SEAL-based applications with a scheduler and a memory map.

On-chip vs off-chip. Off-chip DRAM has gigabytes of capacity but high latency; on-chip BRAM has only megabits but very high throughput. A primary goal is to avoid off-chip access. Thanks to minimal-BRAM techniques, BRAM reuse, and data compaction, no off-chip access is needed for the $n = 2^{12}$ set on either board. For $n = 2^{13}$ there is enough on-chip memory on Stratix 10. For $n = 2^{14}$ there is not enough BRAM, so some data must go to DRAM. HEAX places the key-switching keys (ksk) in DRAM because their size grows fastest (roughly $O(n\,k^2)$, and since $k$ grows roughly linearly with $n$, almost $O(n^3)$) and each ksk element is read only once per KeySwitch, whereas each twiddle factor is read $k$ times. The ksk is distributed across four DRAM banks and read in burst mode, fully pipelined; the authors verify DRAM bandwidth suffices (for $n = 2^{14}$, $k = 8$, 64-bit words, about $151$ megabits must stream in $383$ microseconds, requiring more than $49.28$ GBps, below the combined four-channel bandwidth). DRAM can also cache results destined for reuse, addressed via the host-side memory map without PCIe round trips.

PCIe transfer. Computation and transfer are interleaved. Transfers use DMA over pinned memory; to maximize PCIe throughput, at least a complete polynomial ($2^{15}$ to $2^{17}$ bytes) is sent per request, and eight threads interleave eight polynomials at a time. MULT uses double buffering; KeySwitch needs quadruple buffering due to the input-polynomial dependency, and writing stalls if a buffer has not yet been read.

Implementation, FPGAs, and Parameters

HEAX was implemented on two Intel FPGAs representing different resource classes. Board-A is an Arria 10 GX 1150 ($1518$ DSPs, $1.71$M REG, $427$K ALM, $53$ Mb / $2.7$K M20K BRAM, 2 DRAM channels at $34$ GBps). Board-B is a Stratix 10 GX 2800 ($5760$ DSPs, $3.73$M REG, $933$K ALM, $229$ Mb / $11.7$K M20K BRAM, 4 DRAM channels at $64$ GBps). DSPs perform one 27-bit (or two 18-bit) multiplications; each M20K holds $512$ words of $40$ bits.

Security derives directly from CKKS (unchanged functionality) and uses the HE security standard parameters for 128-bit classical security; changing the word size and using RNS-level parallelism do not affect security. Three parameter sets are evaluated, with $n$ the polynomial size, $\lfloor \log qp \rfloor + 1$ the total ciphertext-modulus bit-length, and $k$ the number of RNS components:

HE Param. Set      n          floor(log qp)+1      k
  Set-A          2^12              109            2
  Set-B          2^13              218            4
  Set-C          2^14              438            8

Sets at $n = 2^{11}$ or below are almost never used (multiplication depth 0 or 1), and $n \ge 2^{15}$ causes enormous blow-up, so these three span the practical range.

The KeySwitch architecture instantiated per board / parameter set (Table 5) shows the multi-level structure explicitly, where $X(c)$ denotes $X$ module(s) with $c$ cores each:

Arria10,   n = 2^12 (Set-A):
  1 x INTT(8)  -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 3 x Dyad(4)
  -> 2 x INTT(4) -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 2 x MS(2)
Stratix10, n = 2^12 (Set-A):
  1 x INTT(16) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 3 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(8) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 2 x MS(4)
Stratix10, n = 2^13 (Set-B):
  1 x INTT(16) -> 4 x NTT(16) -> 5 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(4) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 2 x MS(4)
Stratix10, n = 2^14 (Set-C):
  1 x INTT(8)  -> 4 x NTT(16) -> 5 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(1) -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 2 x MS(4)

Resource Consumption

Per-core resources and pipeline depth (Table 3): the Dyadic core uses 22 DSP, 4526 REG, 1663 ALM, 23 stages; the NTT core 10 DSP, 6297 REG, 2066 ALM, 50 stages; the INTT core 10 DSP, 5449 REG, 2119 ALM, 49 stages.

Module resources scale primarily in DSP / REG / ALM and M20K count with the number of cores (BRAM bits are roughly fixed per module). Selected entries from Table 4 (BRAM reported for Set-B, $n = 2^{13}$):

Module  #Cores  DSP    REG      ALM      BRAM #M20K   Cycles
MULT      4      88   42817    15795        65        1024
MULT      8     176   61878    22160        65         512
MULT     16     352   93594    35257       164         128
MULT     32     704  181503    62157       293          64
NTT       4      40   61670    22316        86        6144
NTT       8      80   96919    36336       185        3072
NTT      16     160  196205    67865       380        1536
NTT      32     320  387357   142300       725         768
INTT      4      40   63917    22700        86        6144
INTT      8      80  104575    37331       185        3072
INTT     16     160  182478    68645       380        1536
INTT     32     320  384267   144957       724         768

Whole-design utilization (Table 6) reaches, for example on Stratix 10 Set-B: $2610$ DSP ($45\%$), $1.98$M REG ($53\%$), $698{,}884$ ALM ($75\%$), BRAM bits $84\%$, M20K $88\%$, at $300$ MHz. Arria 10 Set-A uses $1185$ DSP ($78\%$) at $275$ MHz. The maximum clock frequency after eliminating critical paths is $275$ MHz (Arria 10) and $300$ MHz (Stratix 10). HEAX can be auto-instantiated at different scales with no manual tuning: the Stratix 10 instantiation of Set-A consumes about twice the resources and delivers about twice the throughput of the Arria 10 instantiation.

Performance and Benchmarks

HEAX is compared against Microsoft SEAL V3.3 (single-threaded Intel Xeon Silver 4108 at $1.80$ GHz; SEAL is thread-safe but not multithreaded, and CKKS evaluation is largely non-parallelizable, for example Modulus Switching is serial). Numbers below are operations per second.

Low-level operations (Table 7), CPU vs HEAX and speedup:

FPGA / Set     NTT                 INTT                Dyadic MULT
               CPU    HEAX    x     CPU    HEAX    x      CPU     HEAX      x
Arria10  A    7222   89518  12.4   7568   89518  11.8   36931  1074219   29.1
Stratix  A    7222  195313  27.0   7568  195313  25.8   36931  1171875   31.7
Stratix  B    3437   90144  26.2   3539   90144  25.5   18362   585938   31.9
Stratix  C    1631   41853  25.7   1659   41853  25.2    9117   292969   32.1

High-level operations (Table 8), the headline results:

FPGA / Set     KeySwitch                   MULT+ReLin
               CPU    HEAX    Speed-up      CPU    HEAX    Speed-up
Arria10  A     488   44759     91.7         420   44759    106.6
Stratix  A     488   97656    200.5         420   97656    232.5
Stratix  B      97   22536    232.3          84   22536    268.3
Stratix  C      16    2616    163.5          15    2616    174.4

On Arria 10 HEAX reaches close to two orders of magnitude speedup; on Stratix 10 it achieves $163.5\times$ to $268.3\times$ improvement across parameter sets for the complete high-level operations.

Versus GPUs (Table 9), NTT operations per second: HEAX on Stratix 10 (ten 16-core NTT modules) vs NVIDIA Tesla K80 (2496 cores) and Tesla P100 (3584 cores):

Poly Size    HEAX(10x16)   Tesla-K80   Tesla-P100   Comparison
  2^12         1953130        25641       27777       70-76x
  2^13          901440        20833       25000       36-43x
  2^14          418530         5181       11494       36-81x

HEAX is $36\times$ to $81\times$ faster than data-center GPUs for NTT while consuming significantly less power.

Related Work, Limitations, and Conclusion

No prior FPGA or GPU hardware existed for CKKS, so HEAX is the first; comparison to designs for other (non-CKKS) schemes is not strictly fair. Prior FPGA work for BFV (for example for $n = 2^{15}$) suffered from massive off-chip transfers and did not beat CPUs. The closest work, Roy et al., built a BFV architecture on Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU102, avoiding off-chip access by fixing $n = 2^{12}$ and reporting $13\times$ over FV-NFLlib (which is itself $1.2\times$ slower than SEAL); HEAX is far more modular and scalable, instantiated for three parameter sets with no manual tuning. Other lines include FPGA co-processors accelerating single operations (for example HEPU for LTV, $3.2\times$ to $4.4\times$), very large integer multipliers ($768$K to $1.18$M-bit) for FHE-over-integers, GPU accelerations of various schemes, and YASHE / LTV accelerators, though YASHE and LTV rest on an overstretched NTRU assumption that is broken by a subfield-lattice attack and are no longer secure. Some prior YASHE designs assumed unlimited memory bandwidth (unrealistic) or relied on Cached-NTT; HEAX instead exploits the RNS ring-isomorphism to do independent per-component computation, avoiding off-chip access for small parameters and minimizing it for large ones.

Limitations / scope. HEAX accelerates only the server-side evaluation operations (Addition, Multiplication, Key Switching, Relinearization, Rescaling, Rotation); encoding / decoding and encryption / decryption stay on the client. For the largest parameter set ($n = 2^{14}$) the design cannot keep all data on-chip and must stream the key-switching keys from DRAM, so its throughput depends on DRAM bandwidth being sufficient. Parameters above $n = 2^{15}$ are out of the design's intended range. The comparison baseline (SEAL) is single-threaded, and the GPU comparison is restricted to the NTT primitive rather than full high-level operations.

Conclusion. HEAX is the first fully implemented hardware accelerator for the CKKS FHE scheme, the prime candidate for machine learning on encrypted data. Its components are reusable for other lattice-based cryptosystems and FHE / HE schemes, and the architecture is flexible across FPGA chips and not tied to a fixed parameter set, demonstrating more than two orders of magnitude speedup over optimized CPU software across a wide range of FHE parameters. The authors hope HEAX paves the way for large-scale deployment of privacy-preserving cloud computation.

Algorithms

Complete, self-contained pseudocode for every algorithm HEAX builds on. Notation: $\odot$ is the coefficient-wise (dyadic) product, written (*) below; a tilde marks NTT-domain (transformed) polynomials; $\ell$ indexes the active RNS components, $L+1 = k$ is the full count, and $p$ is the special modulus.

Algorithm 1  Optimized Modular Multiplication  |  MulRed(x, y, y', p)
Input:  x, y in Z_p ;  p < 2^(w-2) ;  precomputed y' = floor( y . 2^w / p )
Output: z = x . y  (mod p)
  1:  z   <-  x . y          (mod 2^w)      // lower word of the product
  2:  t   <-  floor( x . y' / 2^w )         // upper word of the product
  3:  z_e <-  t . p          (mod 2^w)      // lower word of the product
  4:  z   <-  z - z_e                       // single-word subtraction
  5:  if z >= p then
  6:      z <-  z - p
  7:  end if
  8:  return z
Algorithm 2  Homomorphic Multiplication  |  CKKS.Mul(ct0, ct1)
Input:  ct0 = (A0~, A1~),  ct1 = (B0~, B1~)   in ( prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i} )^2
Output: ct  = (C0~, C1~, C2~)                 in ( prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i} )^3
  1:  for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
  2:      c0,i~ <-  Mod( a0,i~ (*) b0,i~ , p_i )                       // Dyadic Core
  3:      c1,i~ <-  Mod( a0,i~ (*) b1,i~ + a1,i~ (*) b0,i~ , p_i )
  4:      c2,i~ <-  Mod( a1,i~ (*) b1,i~ , p_i )
  5:  end for
  6:  return ct = (C0~, C1~, C2~)
Algorithm 3  Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT)  |  NTT_p(a)
Input:  a in Z_p^n ;  p == 1 (mod 2n) ;
        Y in Z_p^n  storing powers of psi in bit-reverse order ;
        Y' = floor( Y . 2^w / p )
Output: a~ = NTT_p(a)  in bit-reverse ordering
  1:  for (m = 1; m < n; m = 2*m) do
  2:      for (i = 0; i < m; i = i + 1) do
  3:          for (j = i*n/m; j < (2*i+1)*n/(2*m); j = j + 1) do
  4:              v          <-  MulRed( a[j + n/m], y[m+i], y'[m+i], p )
  5:              a[j + n/m] <-  a[j] - v     (mod p)
  6:              a[j]       <-  a[j] + v     (mod p)        // NTT Core
  7:          end for
  8:      end for
  9:  end for
 10:  a~ <- a
 11:  return a~
Algorithm 4  RNS Flooring  |  Floor(C~, p)
Input:  C~ = (c0~, ..., c_{l+1}~)  in  Z_{p0}^n x ... x Z_{pl}^n x Z_p^n
Output: C'~ = (c0'~, ..., cl'~)    in  Z_{p0}^n x ... x Z_{pl}^n
  1:  a <-  INTT_p( c_{l+1}~ )                          // INTT Module
  2:  for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
  3:      r   <-  Mod( a, p_i )
  4:      r~  <-  NTT_{p_i}( r )                          // NTT Module
  5:      ci'~ <-  ci~ - r~              (mod p_i)
  6:      ci'~ <-  Mod( [p^{-1}]_{p_i} . ci'~ , p_i )     // MS Module
  7:  end for
  8:  return C'~ = (c0'~, ..., cl'~)
Algorithm 5  Key Switching  |  KeySwitch(ct, ksk)
Input:  ct  = (C0~, C1~)  in ( prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i} )^2
        ksk = ( (Di,0~)_{0<=i<=L+1} | (Di,1~)_{0<=i<=L+1} )
              in ( p . prod_{i=0..L} R_{p_i} )^{(L+2) x 2}
Output: ct' = (C0'~, C1'~)  in ( prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i} )^2
  1:  for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
  2:      a <- INTT_{p_i}( c1,i~ )                              // INTT Module
  3:      for (j = 0; j <= l; j = j + 1) do
  4:          if i != j then
  5:              b  <- Mod( a, p_j )
  6:              b~ <- NTT_{p_j}( b )                            // NTT Module
  7:          else
  8:              b~ <- a~
  9:          end if
 10:          c0,j''~ <- c0,j''~ + b~ (*) d_{i,0,j}~    (mod p_j)
 11:          c1,j''~ <- c1,j''~ + b~ (*) d_{i,1,j}~    (mod p_j)  // Dyadic Mod.
 12:      end for
 13:      b  <- Mod( a, p )
 14:      b~ <- NTT_p( b )                                       // NTT Module
 15:      c0,L+1''~ <- c0,L+1''~ + b~ (*) d_{i,0,L+1}~  (mod p)
 16:      c1,L+1''~ <- c1,L+1''~ + b~ (*) d_{i,1,L+1}~  (mod p)   // Dyadic Mod.
 17:  end for
 18:  ct' <- ( Floor(C0''~, p), Floor(C1''~, p) )               // INTT / NTT / MS
 19:  ct' <- CKKS.Add( ct, ct' )
 20:  return ct'
